### Radford ╡╚ - Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training.pdf
pages 12
Improving Language Understanding
by Generative Pre-Training
Alec Radford
OpenAI
alec@openai.comKarthik Narasimhan
OpenAI
karthikn@openai.comTim Salimans
OpenAI
tim@openai.comIlya Sutskever
OpenAI
ilyasu@openai.com
Abstract
Natural language understanding comprises a wide range of diverse tasks such
as textual entailment, question answering, semantic similarity assessment, and
document classiﬁcation. Although large unlabeled text corpora are abundant,
labeled data for learning these speciﬁc tasks is scarce, making it challenging for
discriminatively trained models to perform adequately. We demonstrate that large
gains on these tasks can be realized by generative pre-training of a language model
on a diverse corpus of unlabeled text, followed by discriminative ﬁne-tuning on each
speciﬁc task. In contrast to previous approaches, we make use of task-aware input
transformations during ﬁne-tuning to achieve effective transfer while requiring
minimal changes to the model architecture. We demonstrate the effectiveness of
our approach on a wide range of benchmarks for natural language understanding.
Our general task-agnostic model outperforms discriminatively trained models that
use architectures speciﬁcally crafted for each task, signiﬁcantly improving upon the
state of the art in 9 out of the 12 tasks studied. For instance, we achieve absolute
improvements of 8.9% on commonsense reasoning (Stories Cloze Test), 5.7% on
question answering (RACE), and 1.5% on textual entailment (MultiNLI).
1 Introduction
The ability to learn effectively from raw text is crucial to alleviating the dependence on supervised
learning in natural language processing (NLP). Most deep learning methods require substantial
amounts of manually labeled data, which restricts their applicability in many domains that suffer
from a dearth of annotated resources [ 61]. In these situations, models that can leverage linguistic
information from unlabeled data provide a valuable alternative to gathering more annotation, which
can be time-consuming and expensive. Further, even in cases where considerable supervision
is available, learning good representations in an unsupervised fashion can provide a signiﬁcant
performance boost. The most compelling evidence for this so far has been the extensive use of pre-
trained word embeddings [ 10,39,42] to improve performance on a range of NLP tasks [ 8,11,26,45].
Leveraging more than word-level information from unlabeled text, however, is challenging for two
main reasons. First, it is unclear what type of optimization objectives are most effective at learning
text representations that are useful for transfer. Recent research has looked at various objectives
such as language modeling [ 44], machine translation [ 38], and discourse coherence [ 22], with each
method outperforming the others on different tasks.1Second, there is no consensus on the most
effective way to transfer these learned representations to the target task. Existing techniques involve
a combination of making task-speciﬁc changes to the model architecture [ 43,44], using intricate
learning schemes [ 21] and adding auxiliary learning objectives [ 50]. These uncertainties have made
it difﬁcult to develop effective semi-supervised learning approaches for language processing.
1https://gluebenchmark.com/leaderboard
Preprint. Work in progress.
In this paper, we explore a semi-supervised approach for language understanding tasks using a
combination of unsupervised pre-training and supervised ﬁne-tuning. Our goal is to learn a universal
representation that transfers with little adaptation to a wide range of tasks. We assume access to
a large corpus of unlabeled text and several datasets with manually annotated training examples
(target tasks). Our setup does not require these target tasks to be in the same domain as the unlabeled
corpus. We employ a two-stage training procedure. First, we use a language modeling objective on
the unlabeled data to learn the initial parameters of a neural network model. Subsequently, we adapt
these parameters to a target task using the corresponding supervised objective.
For our model architecture, we use the Transformer [62], which has been shown to perform strongly on
various tasks such as machine translation [ 62], document generation [ 34], and syntactic parsing [ 29].
This model choice provides us with a more structured memory for handling long-term dependencies in
text, compared to alternatives like recurrent networks, resulting in robust transfer performance across
diverse tasks. During transfer, we utilize task-speciﬁc input adaptations derived from traversal-style
approaches [ 52], which process structured text input as a single contiguous sequence of tokens. As
we demonstrate in our experiments, these adaptations enable us to ﬁne-tune effectively with minimal
changes to the architecture of the pre-trained model.
We evaluate our approach on four types of language understanding tasks – natural language inference,
question answering, semantic similarity, and text classiﬁcation. Our general task-agnostic model
outperforms discriminatively trained models that employ architectures speciﬁcally crafted for each
task, signiﬁcantly improving upon the state of the art in 9 out of the 12 tasks studied. For instance,
we achieve absolute improvements of 8.9% on commonsense reasoning (Stories Cloze Test) [ 40],
5.7% on question answering (RACE) [ 30], 1.5% on textual entailment (MultiNLI) [ 66] and 5.5% on
the recently introduced GLUE multi-task benchmark [ 64]. We also analyzed zero-shot behaviors
of the pre-trained model on four different settings and demonstrate that it acquires useful linguistic
knowledge for downstream tasks.
2 Related Work
Semi-supervised learning for NLP Our work broadly falls under the category of semi-supervised
learning for natural language. This paradigm has attracted signiﬁcant interest, with applications to
tasks like sequence labeling [ 24,33,57] or text classiﬁcation [ 41,70]. The earliest approaches used
unlabeled data to compute word-level or phrase-level statistics, which were then used as features in a
supervised model [33]. Over the last few years, researchers have demonstrated the beneﬁts of using
word embeddings [ 11,39,42], which are trained on unlabeled corpora, to improve performance on a
variety of tasks [ 8,11,26,45]. These approaches, however, mainly transfer word-level information,
whereas we aim to capture higher-level semantics.
Recent approaches have investigated learning and utilizing more than word-level semantics from
unlabeled data. Phrase-level or sentence-level embeddings, which can be trained using an unlabeled
corpus, have been used to encode text into suitable vector representations for various target tasks [ 28,
32, 1, 36, 22, 12, 56, 31].
Unsupervised pre-training Unsupervised pre-training is a special case of semi-supervised learning
where the goal is to ﬁnd a good initialization point instead of modifying the supervised learning
objective. Early works explored the use of the technique in image classiﬁcation [ 20,49,63] and
regression tasks [ 3]. Subsequent research [ 15] demonstrated that pre-training acts as a regularization
scheme, enabling better generalization in deep neural networks. In recent work, the method has
been used to help train deep neural networks on various tasks like image classiﬁcation [ 69], speech
recognition [68], entity disambiguation [17] and machine translation [48].
The closest line of work to ours involves pre-training a neural network using a language modeling
objective and then ﬁne-tuning it on a target task with supervision. Dai et al. [ 13] and Howard and
Ruder [ 21] follow this method to improve text classiﬁcation. However, although the pre-training
phase helps capture some linguistic information, their usage of LSTM models restricts their prediction
ability to a short range. In contrast, our choice of transformer networks allows us to capture longer-
range linguistic structure, as demonstrated in our experiments. Further, we also demonstrate the
effectiveness of our model on a wider range of tasks including natural language inference, paraphrase
detection and story completion. Other approaches [ 43,44,38] use hidden representations from a
2
pre-trained language or machine translation model as auxiliary features while training a supervised
model on the target task. This involves a substantial amount of new parameters for each separate
target task, whereas we require minimal changes to our model architecture during transfer.
Auxiliary training objectives Adding auxiliary unsupervised training objectives is an alternative
form of semi-supervised learning. Early work by Collobert and Weston [ 10] used a wide variety of
auxiliary NLP tasks such as POS tagging, chunking, named entity recognition, and language modeling
to improve semantic role labeling. More recently, Rei [ 50] added an auxiliary language modeling
objective to their target task objective and demonstrated performance gains on sequence labeling
tasks. Our experiments also use an auxiliary objective, but as we show, unsupervised pre-training
already learns several linguistic aspects relevant to target tasks.
3 Framework
Our training procedure consists of two stages. The ﬁrst stage is learning a high-capacity language
model on a large corpus of text. This is followed by a ﬁne-tuning stage, where we adapt the model to
a discriminative task with labeled data.

### Radford ╡╚ - Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners.pdf

Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners
Alec Radford*1Jeffrey Wu*1Rewon Child1David Luan1Dario Amodei**1Ilya Sutskever**1
Abstract
Natural language processing tasks, such as ques-
tion answering, machine translation, reading com-
prehension, and summarization, are typically
approached with supervised learning on task-
speciﬁc datasets. We demonstrate that language
models begin to learn these tasks without any ex-
plicit supervision when trained on a new dataset
of millions of webpages called WebText. When
conditioned on a document plus questions, the an-
swers generated by the language model reach 55
F1 on the CoQA dataset - matching or exceeding
the performance of 3 out of 4 baseline systems
without using the 127,000+ training examples.
The capacity of the language model is essential
to the success of zero-shot task transfer and in-
creasing it improves performance in a log-linear
fashion across tasks. Our largest model, GPT-2,
is a 1.5B parameter Transformer that achieves
state of the art results on 7 out of 8 tested lan-
guage modeling datasets in a zero-shot setting
but still underﬁts WebText. Samples from the
model reﬂect these improvements and contain co-
herent paragraphs of text. These ﬁndings suggest
a promising path towards building language pro-
cessing systems which learn to perform tasks from
their naturally occurring demonstrations.
1. Introduction
Machine learning systems now excel (in expectation) at
tasks they are trained for by using a combination of large
datasets, high-capacity models, and supervised learning
(Krizhevsky et al., 2012) (Sutskever et al., 2014) (Amodei
et al., 2016). Yet these systems are brittle and sensitive to
slight changes in the data distribution (Recht et al., 2018)
and task speciﬁcation (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017). Current sys-
tems are better characterized as narrow experts rather than
*, **Equal contribution1OpenAI, San Francisco, Califor-
nia, United States. Correspondence to: Alec Radford
<alec@openai.com >.competent generalists. We would like to move towards more
general systems which can perform many tasks – eventually
without the need to manually create and label a training
dataset for each one.
The dominant approach to creating ML systems is to col-
lect a dataset of training examples demonstrating correct
behavior for a desired task, train a system to imitate these
behaviors, and then test its performance on independent
and identically distributed (IID) held-out examples. This
has served well to make progress on narrow experts. But
the often erratic behavior of captioning models (Lake et al.,
2017), reading comprehension systems (Jia & Liang, 2017),
and image classiﬁers (Alcorn et al., 2018) on the diversity
and variety of possible inputs highlights some of the short-
comings of this approach.
Our suspicion is that the prevalence of single task training
on single domain datasets is a major contributor to the lack
of generalization observed in current systems. Progress
towards robust systems with current architectures is likely
to require training and measuring performance on a wide
range of domains and tasks. Recently, several benchmarks
have been proposed such as GLUE (Wang et al., 2018) and
decaNLP (McCann et al., 2018) to begin studying this.
Multitask learning (Caruana, 1997) is a promising frame-
work for improving general performance. However, mul-
titask training in NLP is still nascent. Recent work re-
ports modest performance improvements (Yogatama et al.,
2019) and the two most ambitious efforts to date have
trained on a total of 10 and 17 (dataset, objective)
pairs respectively (McCann et al., 2018) (Bowman et al.,
2018). From a meta-learning perspective, each (dataset,
objective) pair is a single training example sampled
from the distribution of datasets and objectives. Current
ML systems need hundreds to thousands of examples to
induce functions which generalize well. This suggests that
multitask training many need just as many effective training
pairs to realize its promise with current approaches. It will
be very difﬁcult to continue to scale the creation of datasets
and the design of objectives to the degree that may be re-
quired to brute force our way there with current techniques.
This motivates exploring additional setups for performing
multitask learning.
The current best performing systems on language tasks

utilize a combination of pre-training and supervised ﬁne-
tuning. This approach has a long history with a trend to-
wards more ﬂexible forms of transfer. First, word vectors
were learned and used as inputs to task-speciﬁc architec-
tures (Mikolov et al., 2013) (Collobert et al., 2011), then
the contextual representations of recurrent networks were
transferred (Dai & Le, 2015) (Peters et al., 2018), and re-
cent work suggests that task-speciﬁc architectures are no
longer necessary and transferring many self-attention blocks
is sufﬁcient (Radford et al., 2018) (Devlin et al., 2018).
These methods still require supervised training in order
to perform a task. When only minimal or no supervised
data is available, another line of work has demonstrated
the promise of language models to perform speciﬁc tasks,
such as commonsense reasoning (Schwartz et al., 2017) and
sentiment analysis (Radford et al., 2017).
In this paper, we connect these two lines of work and con-
tinue the trend of more general methods of transfer. We
demonstrate language models can perform down-stream
tasks in a zero-shot setting – without any parameter or archi-
tecture modiﬁcation. We demonstrate this approach shows
potential by highlighting the ability of language models to
perform a wide range of tasks in a zero-shot setting. We
achieve promising, competitive, and state of the art results
depending on the task.
2. Approach
At the core of our approach is language modeling. Lan-
guage modeling is usually framed as unsupervised distri-
bution estimation from a set of examples (x1; x2; :::; x n)
each composed of variable length sequences of symbols
(s1; s2; :::; s n). Since language has a natural sequential or-
dering, it is common to factorize the joint probabilities oversymbols as the product of conditional probabilities (Jelinek
& Mercer, 1980) (Bengio et al., 2003):
p(x) =nY
i=1p(snjs1; :::; s n1) (1)
This approach allows for tractable sampling from and es-
timation of p(x)as well as any conditionals of the form
p(snk; :::; s njs1; :::; s nk1). In recent years, there have
been signiﬁcant improvements in the expressiveness of mod-
els that can compute these conditional probabilities, such as
self-attention architectures like the Transformer (Vaswani
et al., 2017).
Learning to perform a single task can be expressed in a
probabilistic framework as estimating a conditional distri-
bution p(output jinput ). Since a general system should be
able to perform many different tasks, even for the same
input, it should condition not only on the input but also
on the task to be performed. That is, it should model
p(output jinput; task ). This has been variously formalized
in multitask and meta-learning settings. Task conditioning
is often implemented at an architectural level, such as the
task speciﬁc encoders and decoders in (Kaiser et al., 2017)
or at an algorithmic level such as the inner and outer loop
optimization framework of MAML (Finn et al., 2017). But
as exempliﬁed in McCann et al. (2018), language provides
a ﬂexible way to specify tasks, inputs, and outputs all as a
sequence of symbols. For example, a translation training
example can be written as the sequence (translate to
french, english text, french text) . Like-
wise, a reading comprehension training example can
be written as (answer the question, document,
question, answer) . McCann et al. (2018) demon-
strated it was possible to train a single model, the MQAN,
Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners
to infer and perform many different tasks on examples with
this type of format.
Language modeling is also able to, in principle, learn the
tasks of McCann et al. (2018) without the need for explicit
supervision of which symbols are the outputs to be pre-
dicted. Since the supervised objective is the the same as the
unsupervised objective but only evaluated on a subset of the
sequence, the global minimum of the unsupervised objective
is also the global minimum of the supervised objective. In
this slightly toy setting, the concerns with density estimation
as a principled training objective discussed in (Sutskever
et al., 2015) are side stepped. The problem instead becomes
whether we are able to, in practice, optimize the unsuper-
vised objective to convergence. Preliminary experiments
conﬁrmed that sufﬁciently large language models are able to
perform multitask learning in this toy-ish setup but learning
is much slower than in explicitly supervised approaches.
While it is a large step from the well-posed setup described
above to the messiness of “language in the wild”, Weston
(2016) argues, in the context of dialog, for the need to
develop systems capable of learning from natural language
directly and demonstrated a proof of concept – learning a
QA task without a reward signal by using forward prediction
of a teacher’s outputs. While dialog is an attractive approach,
we worry it is overly restrictive. The internet contains a vast
amount of information that is passively available without
the need for interactive communication. Our speculation is
that a language model with sufﬁcient capacity will begin
to learn to infer and perform the tasks demonstrated in
natural language sequences in order to better predict them,
regardless of their method of procurement. If a language
model is able to do this it will be, in effect, performing
unsupervised multitask learning. We test whether this is the
case by analyzing the performance of language models in a
zero-shot setting on a wide variety of tasks.
2.1. Training Dataset
Most prior work trained language models on a single do-
main of text, such as news articles (Jozefowicz et al., 2016),
Wikipedia (Merity et al., 2016), or ﬁction books (Kiros
et al., 2015). Our approach motivates building as large and
diverse a dataset as possible in order to collect natural lan-
guage demonstrations of tasks in as varied of domains and
contexts as possible.
A promising source of diverse and nearly unlimited text is

HEAD: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners Tom B. BrownBenjamin MannNick RyderMelanie Subbiah Jared KaplanyPrafulla Dhariwal Arvind Neelakantan Pranav Shyam Girish Sastry Amanda Askell Sandhini Agarwal Ariel Herbert-Voss Gretchen Krueger Tom Henighan Rewon Child Aditya Ramesh Daniel M. Ziegler Jeffrey Wu Clemens Winter Christopher Hesse Mark Chen Eric Sigler Mateusz Litwin Scott Gray Benjamin Chess Jack Clark Christopher Berner Sam McCandlish Alec Radford Ilya Sutskever Dario Amodei OpenAI Abstract Recent work has demonstrated substantial gains on many NLP tasks and benchmarks by pre-training on a large corpus of text followed by ﬁne-tuning on a speciﬁc task. While typically task-agnostic in architecture, this method still requires task-speciﬁc ﬁne-tuning datasets of thousands or tens of thousands of examples. By contrast, humans can generally perform a new language task from only a few examples or from simple instructions – something which current NLP systems still largely struggle to do. Here we show that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art ﬁne- tuning approaches. Speciﬁcally, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, 10x more than any previous non-sparse language model, and test its performance in the few-shot setting. For all tasks, GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or ﬁne-tuning, with tasks and few-shot demonstrations speciﬁed purely via text interaction with the model. GPT-3 achieves strong performance on many NLP datasets, including translation, question-answering, and cloze tasks, as well as several tasks that require on-the-ﬂy reasoning or domain adaptation, such as unscrambling words, using a novel word in a sentence, or performing 3-digit arithmetic. At the same time, we also identify some datasets where GPT-3’s few-shot learning still struggles, as well as some datasets where GPT-3 faces methodolo

HEAD: GPT-4 Technical Report OpenAI∗ Abstract We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score around the top 10% of test takers. GPT-4 is a Transformer- based model pre-trained to predict the next token in a document. The post-training alignment process results in improved performance on measures of factuality and adherence to desired behavior. A core component of this project was developing infrastructure and optimization methods that behave predictably across a wide range of scales. This allowed us to accurately predict some aspects of GPT-4’s performance based on models trained with no more than 1/1,000th the compute of GPT-4. 1 Introduction This technical report presents GPT-4, a large multimodal model capable of processing image and text inputs and producing text outputs. Such models are an important area of study as they have the potential to be used in a wide range of applications, such as dialogue systems, text summarization, and machine translation. As such, they have been the subject of substantial interest and progress in recent years [1–34]. One of the main goals of developing such models is to improve their ability to understand and generate natural language text, particularly in more complex and nuanced scenarios. To test its capabilities in such scenarios, GPT-4 was evaluated on a variety of exams originally designed for humans. In these evaluations it performs quite well and often outscores the vast majority of human test takers. For example, on a simulated bar exam, GPT-4 achieves a score that falls in the top 10% of test takers. This contrasts with GPT-3.5, which scores in the bottom 10%. On a suite of traditional NLP benchmarks, GPT-4 outperforms both previous large langua

HEAD: Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training Alec Radford OpenAI alec@openai.comKarthik Narasimhan OpenAI karthikn@openai.comTim Salimans OpenAI tim@openai.comIlya Sutskever OpenAI ilyasu@openai.com Abstract Natural language understanding comprises a wide range of diverse tasks such as textual entailment, question answering, semantic similarity assessment, and document classiﬁcation. Although large unlabeled text corpora are abundant, labeled data for learning these speciﬁc tasks is scarce, making it challenging for discriminatively trained models to perform adequately. We demonstrate that large gains on these tasks can be realized by generative pre-training of a language model on a diverse corpus of unlabeled text, followed by discriminative ﬁne-tuning on each speciﬁc task. In contrast to previous approaches, we make use of task-aware input transformations during ﬁne-tuning to achieve effective transfer while requiring minimal changes to the model architecture. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on a wide range of benchmarks for natural language understanding. Our general task-agnostic model outperforms discriminatively trained models that use architectures speciﬁcally crafted for each task, signiﬁcantly improving upon the state of the art in 9 out of the 12 tasks studied. For instance, we achieve absolute improvements of 8.9% on commonsense reasoning (Stories Cloze Test), 5.7% on question answering (RACE), and 1.5% on textual entailment (MultiNLI). 1 Introduction The ability to learn effectively from raw text is crucial to alleviating the dependence on supervised learning in natural language processing (NLP). Most deep learning methods require substantial amounts of manually labeled data, which restricts their applicability in many domains that suffer from a dearth of annotated resources [ 61]. In these situations, models that can leverage linguistic information from unlabeled data provide a valuable alternative to gathering more annot

HEAD: Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners Alec Radford*1Jeffrey Wu*1Rewon Child1David Luan1Dario Amodei**1Ilya Sutskever**1 Abstract Natural language processing tasks, such as ques- tion answering, machine translation, reading com- prehension, and summarization, are typically approached with supervised learning on task- speciﬁc datasets. We demonstrate that language models begin to learn these tasks without any ex- plicit supervision when trained on a new dataset of millions of webpages called WebText. When conditioned on a document plus questions, the an- swers generated by the language model reach 55 F1 on the CoQA dataset - matching or exceeding the performance of 3 out of 4 baseline systems without using the 127,000+ training examples. The capacity of the language model is essential to the success of zero-shot task transfer and in- creasing it improves performance in a log-linear fashion across tasks. Our largest model, GPT-2, is a 1.5B parameter Transformer that achieves state of the art results on 7 out of 8 tested lan- guage modeling datasets in a zero-shot setting but still underﬁts WebText. Samples from the model reﬂect these improvements and contain co- herent paragraphs of text. These ﬁndings suggest a promising path towards building language pro- cessing systems which learn to perform tasks from their naturally occurring demonstrations. 1. Introduction Machine learning systems now excel (in expectation) at tasks they are trained for by using a combination of large datasets, high-capacity models, and supervised learning (Krizhevsky et al., 2012) (Sutskever et al., 2014) (Amodei et al., 2016). Yet these systems are brittle and sensitive to slight changes in the data distribution (Recht et al., 2018) and task speciﬁcation (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017). Current sys- tems are better characterized as narrow experts rather than *, **Equal contribution1OpenAI, San Francisco, Califor- nia, United States. k- speciﬁc datasets. We demonstrate that language models begin to learn these tasks without any ex- plicit supervision when trained on a new dataset of millions of webpages called WebText. When conditioned on a document plus questions, the an- swers generated by the language model reach 55 F1 on the CoQA dataset - matching or exceeding the performance of 3 out of 4 baseline systems without using the 127,000+ training examples. The capacity of the language model is essential to the success of zero-shot task transfer and in- creasing it improves performance in a log-linear fashion across tasks. 

KW Reddit
eb pages which have been curated/ﬁltered by humans. Manually ﬁltering a full web scrape would be exceptionally expensive so as a starting point, we scraped all outbound links from Reddit, a social media platform, which received at least 3 karma. This can be thought of as a heuristic indicator for whether other users found the link interesting, educational, or just funny. The resulting dataset, WebText, contains the text subset of these 45 million links. To extract the text from HTML responses we use a combination of the Dragnet (Peters & Lecocq, 2013) and Newspaper1content extractors. All re-

This includes natural language inference tasks like the ANLI dataset, and some reading comprehension datasets like RACE or QuAC. By presenting a broad characterization of GPT-3’s strengths and weaknesses, including these limitations, we hope to stimulate study of few-shot learning in language models and draw attention to where progress is most needed. A heuristic sense of the overall results can be seen in Figure 1.3, which aggregates the various tasks (though it should not be seen as a rigorous or meaningful benchmark in itself).

While it has shown some initial promise, this approach still achieves results far inferior to ﬁne-tuning – for example [RWC+19] achieves only 4% on Natural Questions, and even its 55 F1 CoQa result is now more than 35 points behind the state of the art. Meta-learning clearly requires substantial improvement in order to be viable as a practical method of solving language tasks. Another recent trend in language modeling may offer a way forward. In recent years the capacity of transformer language models has increased substantially, from 100 million parameters [ RNSS18 ], to 300 million parameters [DCLT18 ], to 1.5 billion

While typically task-agnostic in architecture, this method still requires task-speciﬁc ﬁne-tuning datasets of thousands or tens of thousands of examples.  NLP benchmarks, GPT-4 outperforms both previous large language models and most state-of-the-art systems (which often have benchmark-specific training or hand-engineering). On the MMLU benchmark [ 35,36], an English-language suite of multiple-choice questions covering 57 subjects, GPT-4 not only outperforms existing models by a considerable margin in English, but also demonstrates strong performance in other languages. On translated variants of MMLU, GPT-4 surpasses the English-language state-of-the-art in 24 of 26 languages considered. We discuss these model capability results, as well as model safety improvements and res

nsiderable margin in English, but also demonstrates strong performance in other languages. On translated variants of MMLU, GPT-4 surpasses the English-language state-of-the-art in 24 of 26 languages considered. We discuss these model capability results, as well as model safety improvements and results, in more detail in later sections. This report also discusses a key challenge of the project, developing deep learning infrastructure and optimization methods that behave predictably across a wide range of scales. This allowed us to make predictions about the expected performance of GPT-4 (based on small runs trained in simil

n many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score around the top 10% of test takers. GPT-4 is a Transformer- based model pre-trained to predict the next token in a document. The post-training alignment process results in improved performance on measures of factuality and adherence to desired behavior. A core component of this project was developing infrastructure and optimization methods that behave predictably across a wide range of scales. {"path":"/tmp/isolated_workspace_1091/papers_summary.md","content":"# Multi-Paper Analysis Summary Report

## Papers Covered
1. **Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training** — Alec Radford, Karthik Narasimhan, Tim Salimans, Ilya Sutskever (OpenAI, 2018)
2. **Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners** — Alec Radford, Jeffrey Wu, Rewon Child, David Luan, Dario Amodei, Ilya Sutskever (OpenAI, 2019)
3. **Language Models are Few-Shot Learners** — Tom B. Brown et al. (OpenAI, 2020)
4. **GPT-4 Technical Report** — OpenAI (2024 version of 2023 report)

---

## 1. Overview Section

### 1.1 Research Field Overview
These four papers trace a major arc in modern large language model (LLM) research: the transition from **task-specific supervised NLP systems** toward **general-purpose generative pretraining**, then to **zero-shot and few-shot task transfer**, and finally to **multimodal, aligned, safety-conscious frontier models**.

At a high level, the papers cover four stages of technical evolution:
- **GPT (2018):** establish generative pretraining + discriminative fine-tuning as a strong transfer-learning paradigm for NLP.
- **GPT-2 (2019):** show that sufficiently large language models trained on broad web data can perform multiple tasks in a **zero-shot** manner without parameter updates.
- **GPT-3 (2020):** demonstrate that scaling model size and data further unlocks strong **in-context learning** and **few-shot adaptation** through prompting alone.
- **GPT-4 (2024 technical report):** extend the paradigm toward **multimodal capability**, **predictable scaling**, and **post-training alignment/safety engineering**, while intentionally withholding some implementation details.

Together, the papers represent the rise of the autoregressive Transformer as a unified foundation for language understanding, reasoning, generation, and generalist task transfer.

### 1.2 Relationship Analysis Between Papers

#### Technical evolution
1. **From representation transfer to foundation models**
   - GPT (2018) still relies on supervised fine-tuning for each downstream task.
   - GPT-2 (2019) weakens that dependence by showing that a single pretrained model can solve multiple tasks zero-shot.
   - GPT-3 (2020) largely replaces task-specific fine-tuning with prompt-based task specification.
   - GPT-4 adds multimodal processing and alignment-centric deployment, making the model not just a research artifact but a safer production system.

2. **From narrow benchmarks to broad capability evaluation**
   - GPT (2018) focuses on classic NLU benchmarks such as entailment, QA, similarity, and classification.
   - GPT-2 expands toward zero-shot reading comprehension, translation, summarization, and language modeling benchmarks.
   - GPT-3 broadens evaluation dramatically to cloze tasks, QA, translation, commonsense reasoning, reading comprehension, SuperGLUE, NLI, arithmetic, and synthetic tasks.
   - GPT-4 emphasizes professional exams, multilingual MMLU, coding capability, multimodal inputs, and safety/system-level concerns.

3. **From architecture transparency to selective disclosure**
   - GPT and GPT-2 provide concrete architectural details.
   - GPT-3 still discloses model sizes, data mixture, and training setup.
   - GPT-4 intentionally withholds model size, dataset composition details, and training compute due to competitive and safety considerations.

#### Method comparison
- **GPT (2018):** pretrain decoder-only Transformer on BooksCorpus, then fine-tune per task.
- **GPT-2 (2019):** train larger decoder-only Transformers on WebText and evaluate zero-shot by formatting tasks as natural language contexts.
- **GPT-3 (2020):** scale to 175B parameters and treat tasks as in-context demonstrations with zero/one/few-shot prompting.
- **GPT-4 (2024 report):** maintain Transformer-based next-token pretraining, add multimodal input handling and RLHF-style post-training for better factuality and behavior.

#### Complementary relationships
- GPT (2018) provides the transfer-learning foundation.
- GPT-2 shows emergent multitask behavior from scale and diverse data.
- GPT-3 turns emergence into a practical prompting interface.
- GPT-4 reframes the frontier around reliability, alignment, deployment, multimodality, and predictability rather than only raw benchmark gains.

### 1.3 Overall Research Trend Summary
The trend across these papers is clear:
- **More scale** → larger parameter counts, broader training corpora, stronger transfer.
- **Less task-specific supervision** → from fine-tuning to zero-shot to few-shot prompting.
- **More generality** → from text classification and QA to reasoning, coding, and multimodal input.
- **More systems engineering** → training infrastructure, scaling laws, safety mitigation, evaluation governance.
- **More concern with limitations** → contamination, bias, hallucinations, misuse, reliability, and transparency trade-offs become central.

The overall direction is toward **foundation models that learn general capabilities from large-scale predictive training and are then shaped by prompting and alignment rather than task-specific retraining**.

---

## 2. Detailed Analysis of Each Paper

# Paper 1: Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training

## 2.1 Basic Information
- **Title:** Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training
- **Authors:** Alec Radford, Karthik Narasimhan, Tim Salimans, Ilya Sutskever
- **Venue:** Preprint / OpenAI report
- **Year:** 2018

## 2.2 Research Background and Motivation
Before this work, NLP progress was often driven by task-specific architectures and substantial labeled data. Word embeddings and contextual encoders had already shown value, but transfer learning in NLP was still fragmented. Different objectives and transfer mechanisms worked inconsistently across tasks, and many systems still needed customized output heads or architectures.

The paper is motivated by two key problems:
1. **Labeled data scarcity** in many language understanding tasks.
2. **Lack of a unified transfer framework** capable of reusing general linguistic knowledge from unlabeled text.

The authors propose that a generative language modeling objective can learn transferable features from raw text, and that these features can then be adapted efficiently to supervised downstream tasks.

## 2.3 Core Contributions
1. **Introduced the GPT pretrain-then-fine-tune paradigm**
   - The paper established a two-stage recipe: unsupervised generative pretraining followed by supervised fine-tuning.
2. **Showed decoder-only Transformers can serve as universal NLP backbones**
   - Instead of hand-crafted architectures for each task, a single pretrained Transformer can be adapted broadly.
3. **Proposed task-aware input transformations**
   - Structured inputs such as premise-hypothesis or question-passage pairs are converted into token sequences suitable for the same model.
4. **Demonstrated strong benchmark transfer**
   - The model improved state of the art on 9 of 12 tasks.
5. **Showed auxiliary LM loss helps fine-tuning**
   - Jointly using supervised loss and language modeling regularizes adaptation and stabilizes training.

## 2.4 Method Details
### Overall approach
The method has two stages:
1. **Unsupervised pretraining** on BooksCorpus using next-token prediction.
2. **Supervised fine-tuning** on downstream tasks using the pretrained model plus a lightweight output layer.

### Model architecture
- Decoder-only Transformer
- 12 layers
- 768-dimensional hidden states
- 12 attention heads
- 3072-dimensional feed-forward inner layer

### Training data
- **BooksCorpus**, with over 7,000 unpublished books
- Chosen because it contains long contiguous spans of text, useful for learning long-range dependencies

### Fine-tuning strategy
The final hidden state is fed into a task-specific classifier. Different tasks are expressed as linearized token sequences. For example:
- text classification → direct input sequence
- entailment → premise and hypothesis concatenated with delimiters
- QA / commonsense tasks → context-question-answer option sequence scoring

An auxiliary language modeling objective is retained during fine-tuning:
- supervised objective + weighted LM objective

This is important because it preserves useful language modeling behavior and reduces overfitting, especially when downstream data is limited.

## 2.5 Experiment Analysis
### Datasets and task categories
The paper evaluates on:
- **Natural language inference:** SNLI, MultiNLI, QNLI, RTE, SciTail
- **Question answering / commonsense:** RACE, Story Cloze
- **Sentence similarity:** MSRP, QQP, STS-B
- **Classification:** SST-2, CoLA

### Evaluation metrics
- Accuracy for most classification-style tasks
- Pearson/Spearman-type measures for semantic similarity benchmarks where appropriate

### Main results
- State-of-the-art improvement on **9 of 12 tasks**
- Reported absolute gains include:
  - **+8.9%** on Story Cloze
  - **+5.7%** on RACE
  - **+1.5%** on MultiNLI

### Key empirical observations
- Pretraining is crucial; training from scratch is much worse.
- More transferred layers generally improve downstream performance.
- Zero-shot behavior emerges to some extent during pretraining, but supervised fine-tuning remains essential.

## 2.6 Strengths and Weaknesses
### Strengths
- Elegant and general training recipe
- Minimal task-specific architectural engineering
- Clear empirical demonstration that generative pretraining transfers well
- Important conceptual bridge from unsupervised LM to supervised NLU

### Weaknesses
- Still strongly dependent on downstream fine-tuning
- Limited scale compared with later GPT models
- Restricted pretraining data diversity relative to web-scale corpora
- Zero-shot capability remains weak

## 2.7 Personal Insights
This paper is historically decisive. Its biggest contribution is not merely benchmark improvement, but the introduction of a reusable paradigm that later became the basis of foundation models. One limitation is that the paper still thinks in terms of task-specific adaptation rather than direct task execution through prompting. In hindsight, it can be seen as the first major step toward in-context learning, but not yet the final form.

Potential improvements from a modern perspective:
- larger and more diverse corpora
- longer context windows
- broader multitask evaluation
- explicit analysis of bias, contamination, and calibration

## 2.8 Per-Paper Summary
The paper established the generative pretraining + fine-tuning recipe and proved that a single decoder-only Transformer pretrained on raw text can transfer strongly across NLP benchmarks.

---

# Paper 2: Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners

## 2.1 Basic Information
- **Title:** Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners
- **Authors:** Alec Radford, Jeffrey Wu, Rewon Child, David Luan, Dario Amodei, Ilya Sutskever
- **Venue:** OpenAI technical report / blog-era release paper
- **Year:** 2019

## 2.2 Research Background and Motivation
Although GPT (2018) showed strong transfer via fine-tuning, the field still relied heavily on supervised datasets for each task. This paper asks a more radical question: **Can a language model learn to perform tasks directly from natural language context without explicit supervision?**

The motivation is that narrow supervised datasets produce brittle systems. If the internet contains naturally occurring demonstrations of translation, QA, summarization, and reasoning, then training on a large enough and diverse enough corpus might induce a model to perform these tasks zero-shot.

## 2.3 Core Contributions
1. **Introduced GPT-2 and demonstrated zero-shot transfer at scale**
   - The central claim is that sufficiently large language models can perform many tasks without task-specific fine-tuning.
2. **Created the WebText dataset**
   - A curated high-quality web corpus derived from Reddit outbound links with at least 3 karma.
3. **Showed scaling drives capability emergence**
   - Larger models exhibit log-linear improvements across many tasks.
4. **Established strong zero-shot language modeling performance**
   - GPT-2 achieved state-of-the-art results on 7 of 8 language modeling datasets in zero-shot settings.
5. **Reframed multitask learning as next-token prediction**
   - Task specification, input, and output can all be embedded in plain language context.

## 2.4 Method Details
### Data
- **WebText** built from text extracted from Reddit outbound links with at least 3 karma
- Preliminary version after cleaning contains **slightly over 8 million documents** and **40 GB of text**
- Wikipedia removed to reduce overlap with common evaluation benchmarks

### Model architecture
The paper scales the decoder-only Transformer family:
- 117M parameters — 12 layers, d_model 768
- 345M parameters — 24 layers, d_model 1024
- 762M parameters — 36 layers, d_model 1280
- **1542M (1.5B) parameters** — 48 layers, d_model 1600

### Input representation
- Byte-level BPE tokenizer
- Same overall autoregressive next-token objective
- Tasks are formatted as text prompts or naturally occurring contexts

### Inference paradigm
No parameter updates are made for downstream tasks. Instead, the model is conditioned on prompts that imply the task, such as:
- reading comprehension context + question
- translation phrase pairs
- summarization style prompts

## 2.5 Experiment Analysis
### Benchmarks covered
- Language modeling: LAMBADA, CBT, WikiText-2, PTB, enwik8, text8, WikiText-103, 1BW
- Reading comprehension: CoQA
- Translation: WMT-14 Fr-En
- Summarization: CNN/DailyMail
- QA: Natural Questions

### Evaluation setup
The emphasis is on **zero-shot** transfer and plain prompting, not fine-tuning.

### Main results
- State of the art on **7 of 8** tested language modeling datasets in zero-shot setting
- **CoQA: 55 F1**, matching or beating 3 out of 4 baselines without using the 127k+ supervised training examples
- Strong gains as model size increases, suggesting emergence rather than simple memorization of narrow tasks

### Important findings
- Capacity is essential: larger models consistently perform better.
- The model still underfits WebText, implying additional headroom from more scale.
- Zero-shot transfer is promising but uneven: some tasks improve sharply, others remain difficult.

## 2.6 Strengths and Weaknesses
### Strengths
- Landmark demonstration of zero-shot transfer
- Strong argument for data quality over raw web quantity
- Excellent evidence that task behavior can emerge from language modeling alone
- Important move from benchmark-specific training toward foundation models

### Weaknesses
- Many zero-shot results still lag specialized supervised systems
- Evaluation prompt design can strongly affect performance
- Limited reliability and controllability
- No systematic safety/alignment framework yet

## 2.7 Personal Insights
GPT-2 is the paper where the field starts to realize that language models may be more than feature extractors or pretraining initializers. It makes the key conceptual leap that multitask behavior can emerge directly from next-token prediction over sufficiently rich text. The WebText construction is also notable: it prioritizes quality and human-curated usefulness, foreshadowing the importance of data curation in later LLM systems.

Potential improvements:
- more explicit evaluation of bias and factuality
- better prompting protocols and calibration
- analysis of contamination risks
- stronger reasoning and instruction-following evaluation

## 2.8 Per-Paper Summary
This paper demonstrates that scaling a decoder-only LM on curated web text yields broad zero-shot multitask behavior, marking the shift from transfer learning via fine-tuning to task execution via prompting.

---

# Paper 3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners

## 2.1 Basic Information
- **Title:** Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
- **Authors:** Tom B. Brown et al.
- **Venue:** NeurIPS 2020 / OpenAI paper (widely circulated via arXiv)
- **Year:** 2020

## 2.2 Research Background and Motivation
GPT-2 showed promising zero-shot multitask behavior, but it still trailed fine-tuned systems on many benchmarks. This paper asks whether **massive scaling** can close that gap and whether models can learn tasks from a small number of demonstrations provided at inference time.

The core motivation is to reduce dependence on supervised fine-tuning datasets and move toward systems that adapt from instructions and examples the way humans often do.

## 2.3 Core Contributions
1. **Introduced GPT-3 at 175B parameters**
   - At the time, the largest dense language model by a wide margin.
2. **Systematically formalized in-context learning**
   - Compared zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting across a wide task suite.
3. **Showed scale dramatically improves few-shot performance**
   - Few-shot prompting becomes competitive with or approaches fine-tuned systems on many tasks.
4. **Expanded benchmark breadth significantly**
   - Included QA, translation, NLI, commonsense reasoning, reading comprehension, SuperGLUE, synthetic tasks, and more.
5. **Analyzed contamination, memorization, and broader impacts**
   - The paper addresses benchmark leakage and societal risks more explicitly than earlier GPT papers.

## 2.4 Method Details
### Model family
The paper trains 8 GPT-3 model sizes, culminating in:
- **GPT-3 175B**
- 96 layers
- hidden size 12288
- 96 attention heads
- context window **2048**

### Training data mixture
The model is trained for **300 billion tokens** on a weighted mixture including:
- **Common Crawl (filtered): 410B tokens, 60% weight**
- **WebText2: 19B tokens, 22% weight**
- **Books1: 12B tokens, 8% weight**
- **Books2: 55B tokens, 8% weight**
- **Wikipedia: 3B tokens, 3% weight**

### Learning paradigm
No gradient updates are used during evaluation.
Tasks are specified through:
- instructions
- one example
- multiple demonstrations

This creates an explicit spectrum:
- fine-tuning
- zero-shot
- one-shot
- few-shot

### Conceptual contribution
The paper popularizes **in-context learning**: the model uses the prompt as a temporary task description and adapts within the forward pass rather than by changing parameters.

## 2.5 Experiment Analysis
### Major benchmark groups
- Language modeling and cloze: PTB, LAMBADA, StoryCloze, HellaSwag
- Closed-book QA: TriviaQA and others
- Translation
- Winograd-style tasks
- Commonsense reasoning: e.g., PIQA, ARC variants
- Reading comprehension: CoQA, DROP, QuAC, SQuADv2, RACE
- SuperGLUE
- NLI
- Synthetic arithmetic and word manipulation tasks

### Selected results highlighted in the paper
- **TriviaQA closed-book:** up to **71.2%** few-shot accuracy
- **CoQA:** 81.5 zero-shot, 84.0 one-shot, **85.0 few-shot F1**
- **LAMBADA:** **86.4% few-shot accuracy**, surpassing prior SOTA
- Strong prompt-based gains on translation, cloze, and several reasoning tasks

### Key negative findings
- GPT-3 still struggles on some reading comprehension datasets such as **RACE** and **QuAC**.
- NLI and adversarial tasks like **ANLI** remain difficult.
- Few-shot performance is not uniformly better than one-shot or zero-shot; prompting remains sensitive.

## 2.6 Strengths and Weaknesses
### Strengths
- Landmark scaling result demonstrating practical few-shot learning
- Broad and transparent benchmark suite
- Strong conceptual framing of in-context meta-learning
- More explicit discussion of bias, energy use, and misuse than predecessors

### Weaknesses
- Very high training cost and environmental footprint
- Performance remains inconsistent across robust reasoning tasks
- Prompt sensitivity complicates fair evaluation
- Large web-corpus training introduces contamination and bias risk

## 2.7 Personal Insights
GPT-3 is the turning point where prompting becomes a first-class programming interface for general AI systems. Its real significance is not just better scores, but the operational shift: instead of building a new model for each task, users increasingly build prompts. The paper also exposes an important truth that remains relevant: scale brings emergent capabilities, but not reliability. The system is powerful, yet brittle in adversarial, calibration-sensitive, or distribution-shifted conditions.

Potential improvements:
- retrieval augmentation for factuality
- better prompt robustness and calibration
- stronger long-context handling
- safer deployment controls
- more systematic reasoning scaffolds

## 2.8 Per-Paper Summary
The paper shows that very large-scale autoregressive LMs can perform many tasks from prompts and demonstrations alone, making in-context learning a central paradigm for LLM use.

---

# Paper 4: GPT-4 Technical Report

## 2.1 Basic Information
- **Title:** GPT-4 Technical Report
- **Authors:** OpenAI
- **Venue:** Technical report / arXiv version
- **Year:** 2024 version of the 2023 report

## 2.2 Research Background and Motivation
By the time of GPT-4, the key question is no longer whether LLMs scale, but how to make frontier models:
- more capable
- more multimodal
- more predictable to train
- safer to deploy

The report is motivated by both capability expansion and deployment risk. The model is intended as a production-grade system rather than only an academic benchmark submission.

## 2.3 Core Contributions
1. **Presented GPT-4 as a large multimodal model**
   - Accepts image and text inputs and outputs text.
2. **Demonstrated frontier-level benchmark performance**
   - Human-level or near-human-level performance on multiple professional and academic exams.
3. **Introduced predictable scaling emphasis**
   - OpenAI reports accurate prediction of final loss and some capabilities from much smaller runs.
4. **Highlighted post-training alignment improvements**
   - RLHF-style post-training improves factuality and adherence to desired behavior.
5. **Integrated safety into the report structure**
   - Risks, system card, adversarial testing, and mitigation become primary content rather than afterthoughts.

## 2.4 Method Details
### High-level training recipe
- Transformer-based next-token prediction pretraining
- Uses publicly available and licensed data
- Post-training with **Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)**

### Multimodality
The model can process:
- text input
- image input
and produce text output

### Predictable scaling framework
A major technical theme is developing infrastructure that behaves predictably across scales. The report presents:
- power-law-style prediction of final loss on an internal codebase dataset
- accurate capability prediction on a subset of HumanEval from models trained with much less compute

### Disclosure limits
Unlike GPT-3, the report intentionally withholds:
- model size
- exact architecture details
- hardware details
- dataset construction specifics
- training compute

This is framed as a trade-off between scientific openness, competitive concerns, and safety.

## 2.5 Experiment Analysis
### Benchmark categories
- Academic and professional exams
- Traditional NLP benchmarks
- Coding benchmarks such as HumanEval
- Multilingual MMLU
- Vision-language examples and benchmark references

### Representative results
- **Uniform Bar Exam:** **298/400 (~90th percentile)**
- Strong gains over GPT-3.5 on major exams
- **MMLU:** outperforms prior LMs substantially in English
- On translated MMLU, **surpasses English-language SOTA in 24 of 26 languages**

### Experimental interpretation
The report emphasizes not only absolute performance but also:
- capability forecasting
- robustness of scaling infrastructure
- safety evaluation
- deployment-aware testing

### Key limitations explicitly stated
- hallucinations / unreliability
- limited context window
- does not learn from experience after deployment
- safety risks including bias, over-reliance, disinformation, privacy, and cybersecurity misuse

## 2.6 Strengths and Weaknesses
### Strengths
- Broad capability leap including multimodality
- Strong integration of systems and safety thinking
- Predictable scaling is highly valuable for frontier training governance
- Better behavior alignment than earlier GPT generations

### Weaknesses
- Reduced scientific transparency
- Many architecture/training details omitted, limiting reproducibility
- Hallucinations and reliability problems persist
- Safety reporting is informative, but full independent verification remains limited

## 2.7 Personal Insights
GPT-4 marks a shift from “bigger benchmark model” to “frontier socio-technical system.” The technical novelty lies not only in raw capability, but in engineering discipline, predictability, multimodal interface design, and alignment workflow. It is also the clearest sign that the field has entered a stage where openness, safety, productization, and governance interact directly with model design.

Potential improvements:
- stronger verifiability and provenance mechanisms
- lower hallucination rates via retrieval or tool use
- more transparent third-party auditing
- better long-context and continual adaptation mechanisms

## 2.8 Per-Paper Summary
GPT-4 extends the GPT line into multimodal, aligned, and deployment-oriented frontier modeling, while making predictable scaling and safety central engineering goals.

---

## 3. Cross-Paper Comparative Analysis

### 3.1 Method Comparison Table

| Dimension | GPT (2018) | GPT-2 (2019) | GPT-3 (2020) | GPT-4 Report (2024) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Core paradigm | Pretrain + fine-tune | Zero-shot transfer | In-context few-shot learning | Multimodal aligned foundation model |
| Supervision at downstream stage | Required | None | None | None for many uses; post-training alignment applied globally |
| Architecture disclosure | Detailed | Detailed | Detailed | Limited |
| Model scale | Small by modern standards | Up to 1.5B | Up to 175B | Undisclosed frontier scale |
| Input modalities | Text | Text | Text | Text + image |
| Main data source | BooksCorpus | WebText | Common Crawl + WebText2 + Books + Wikipedia | Public + licensed data |
| Task interface | Task-specific fine-tuning | Prompting / context formatting | Zero/one/few-shot prompting | Prompting + multimodal input + aligned assistant behavior |
| Main evaluation focus | NLU benchmarks | Zero-shot multitask ability | Broad few-shot benchmark suite | Exams, multilinguality, coding, safety |
| Key technical theme | Transfer learning | Emergent zero-shot behavior | Scale and in-context learning | Predictable scaling + alignment + safety |

### 3.2 Performance Comparison
Because these papers use different benchmark suites and evaluation modes, direct numerical comparison is imperfect. Still, several trends are clear:
- **GPT (2018)** beats task-specific baselines after fine-tuning on classic NLU tasks.
- **GPT-2 (2019)** demonstrates promising zero-shot performance, especially in LM benchmarks and CoQA.
- **GPT-3 (2020)** substantially strengthens prompt-only performance, often approaching or surpassing prior fine-tuned systems on selected tasks such as LAMBADA and closed-book TriviaQA.
- **GPT-4 (2024 report)** surpasses earlier generations on broad academic, coding, and multilingual benchmarks, while adding multimodal capability.

### 3.3 Applicable Scenario Analysis
- **GPT (2018):** best for research settings where labeled downstream data is available and task-specific fine-tuning is acceptable.
- **GPT-2 (2019):** useful when no task-specific labels exist and zero-shot generalization is needed, though reliability is modest.
- **GPT-3 (2020):** well suited to flexible prompt-based applications, rapid prototyping, and low-data tasks.
- **GPT-4 (2024 report):** best for high-capability interactive systems, multimodal assistants, coding help, multilingual applications, and safety-sensitive deployment pipelines—though verification is still necessary.

### 3.4 Evolutionary Interpretation
A concise interpretation of the progression is:
- **GPT:** language models can transfer.
- **GPT-2:** language models can multitask.
- **GPT-3:** language models can learn from prompts.
- **GPT-4:** language models can become multimodal products, but safety and reliability become central bottlenecks.

---

## 4. Summary and Outlook

### 4.1 Key Technical Points Summary
1. **Autoregressive Transformer pretraining is a scalable general-purpose learning framework.**
2. **Data quality and diversity are crucial for emergent task behavior.**
3. **Scale changes not only performance magnitude but also the interaction paradigm.**
   - Fine-tuning gave way to zero-shot prompting, then few-shot in-context learning.
4. **Prompting acts as a lightweight task programming interface.**
5. **Frontier-model development now requires systems engineering, safety evaluation, and post-training alignment.**
6. **Capability gains do not eliminate core limitations** such as hallucinations, bias, prompt sensitivity, and evaluation contamination.

### 4.2 Future Research Direction Suggestions
1. **Reliability and factuality**
   - Reduce hallucinations via retrieval, tool use, verification, and uncertainty calibration.
2. **Reasoning robustness**
   - Improve adversarial generalization, compositional reasoning, and out-of-distribution performance.
3. **Efficient scaling**
   - Achieve frontier capability with lower energy cost and more efficient architectures/training methods.
4. **Long-context and memory mechanisms**
   - Extend useful context length and support persistent, trustworthy adaptation.
5. **Multimodal grounding**
   - Strengthen integration across text, image, audio, video, and structured tools.
6. **Safety, governance, and transparency**
   - Build better audit frameworks, third-party evaluation pipelines, provenance tools, and deployment guardrails.
7. **Contamination-resistant evaluation**
   - Design benchmarks that better distinguish memorization, prompting artifacts, and true generalization.

### 4.3 Final Perspective
These four papers collectively document one of the most important shifts in AI research: moving from specialized NLP models toward general foundation models whose capabilities emerge from scale, data diversity, and generative training. At the same time, they reveal a parallel truth: as models become more general and useful, the hardest problems increasingly shift from raw capability to **reliability, interpretability, safety, and governance**.
"}

{"path":"/tmp/isolated_workspace_1091/generate_ppt.py","content":"import json
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
from pathlib import Path

from pptx import Presentation
from pptx.enum.shapes import MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE
from pptx.enum.text import PP_ALIGN
from pptx.dml.color import RGBColor
from pptx.util import Inches, Pt

BASE_DIR = Path(__file__).resolve().parent
OUTPUT = BASE_DIR / "papers_analysis.pptx"
STRUCTURE = BASE_DIR / "ppt_structure.json"
SUMMARY = BASE_DIR / "papers_summary.md"

# Theme
NAVY = RGBColor(13, 38, 76)
BLUE = RGBColor(29, 78, 137)
ACCENT = RGBColor(56, 132, 255)
LIGHT = RGBColor(244, 247, 252)
WHITE = RGBColor(255, 255, 255)
TEXT = RGBColor(36, 41, 46)
MUTED = RGBColor(94, 106, 120)
BORDER = RGBColor(180, 191, 204)
GOOD = RGBColor(42, 128, 94)
WARN = RGBColor(184, 91, 44)

prs = Presentation()
prs.slide_width = Inches(13.333)
prs.slide_height = Inches(7.5)


def add_bg(slide, color=LIGHT):
    fill = slide.background.fill
    fill.solid()
    fill.fore_color.rgb = color


def add_header_bar(slide, title, subtitle=None, dark=False):
    top_color = NAVY if dark else BLUE
    fg = WHITE if dark else WHITE
    rect = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.RECTANGLE, 0, 0, prs.slide_width, Inches(0.85))
    rect.fill.solid()
    rect.fill.fore_color.rgb = top_color
    rect.line.color.rgb = top_color

    tx = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(0.45), Inches(0.16), Inches(10.8), Inches(0.36))
    p = tx.text_frame.paragraphs[0]
    p.text = title
    p.font.size = Pt(28)
    p.font.bold = True
    p.font.color.rgb = fg

    if subtitle:
        sb = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(10.2), Inches(0.18), Inches(2.6), Inches(0.28))
        p2 = sb.text_frame.paragraphs[0]
        p2.text = subtitle
        p2.alignment = PP_ALIGN.RIGHT
        p2.font.size = Pt(12)
        p2.font.color.rgb = WHITE


def add_footer(slide, idx):
    line = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.RECTANGLE, Inches(0.35), Inches(7.05), Inches(12.65), Inches(0.02))
    line.fill.solid()
    line.fill.fore_color.rgb = BORDER
    line.line.color.rgb = BORDER
    page = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(12.2), Inches(7.08), Inches(0.7), Inches(0.2))
    p = page.text_frame.paragraphs[0]
    p.text = str(idx)
    p.alignment = PP_ALIGN.RIGHT
    p.font.size = Pt(11)
    p.font.color.rgb = MUTED


def add_bullets(slide, title, bullets, x=0.6, y=1.15, w=12.1, h=5.5, font_size=19):
    box = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(x), Inches(y), Inches(w), Inches(h))
    tf = box.text_frame
    tf.word_wrap = True
    tf.clear()
    for i, item in enumerate(bullets):
        p = tf.paragraphs[0] if i == 0 else tf.add_paragraph()
        if isinstance(item, tuple):
            text, level = item
        else:
            text, level = item, 0
        p.text = text
        p.level = level
        p.font.size = Pt(font_size if level == 0 else max(font_size - 2, 14))
        p.font.color.rgb = TEXT
        p.space_after = Pt(8)
        if level == 0:
            p.bullet = True
    return box


def add_title_only_slide(title, subtitle_lines, slide_no):
    slide = prs.slides.add_slide(prs.slide_layouts[6])
    add_bg(slide, NAVY)
    band = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.RECTANGLE, Inches(0.65), Inches(1.0), Inches(12.0), Inches(5.2))
    band.fill.solid()
    band.fill.fore_color.rgb = BLUE
    band.line.color.rgb = BLUE

    tb = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(1.05), Inches(1.45), Inches(11.2), Inches(1.2))
    p = tb.text_frame.paragraphs[0]
    p.text = title
    p.font.size = Pt(32)
    p.font.bold = True
    p.font.color.rgb = WHITE

    info = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(1.1), Inches(3.0), Inches(10.8), Inches(2.3))
    tf = info.text_frame
    tf.word_wrap = True
    for i, line in enumerate(subtitle_lines):
        p = tf.paragraphs[0] if i == 0 else tf.add_paragraph()
        p.text = line
        p.font.size = Pt(20 if i == 0 else 18)
        p.font.color.rgb = WHITE
        p.space_after = Pt(10)
    add_footer(slide, slide_no)


def add_table_slide(title, columns, rows, slide_no, col_widths=None, subtitle=None):
    slide = prs.slides.add_slide(prs.slide_layouts[6])
    add_bg(slide)
    add_header_bar(slide, title, subtitle)
    nrows = len(rows) + 1
    ncols = len(columns)
    left, top, width, height = Inches(0.45), Inches(1.18), Inches(12.35), Inches(5.65)
    table = slide.shapes.add_table(nrows, ncols, left, top, width, height).table
    if col_widths:
        for i, cw in enumerate(col_widths):
            table.columns[i].width = Inches(cw)
    for i, col in enumerate(columns):
        cell = table.cell(0, i)
        cell.text = col
        cell.fill.solid()
        cell.fill.fore_color.rgb = NAVY
        for p in cell.text_frame.paragraphs:
            p.font.size = Pt(13)
            p.font.bold = True
            p.font.color.rgb = WHITE
            p.alignment = PP_ALIGN.CENTER
    for r, row in enumerate(rows, start=1):
        for c, val in enumerate(row):
            cell = table.cell(r, c)
            cell.text = str(val)
            cell.fill.solid()
            cell.fill.fore_color.rgb = WHITE if r % 2 else RGBColor(235, 241, 249)
            for p in cell.text_frame.paragraphs:
                p.font.size = Pt(12)
                p.font.color.rgb = TEXT
                p.alignment = PP_ALIGN.CENTER if c != 0 else PP_ALIGN.LEFT
    add_footer(slide, slide_no)


def add_two_column_slide(title, left_title, left_bullets, right_title, right_bullets, slide_no, subtitle=None):
    slide = prs.slides.add_slide(prs.slide_layouts[6])
    add_bg(slide)
    add_header_bar(slide, title, subtitle)

    for x, sec_title, bullets in [(0.55, left_title, left_bullets), (6.75, right_title, right_bullets)]:
        card = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.ROUNDED_RECTANGLE, Inches(x), Inches(1.25), Inches(5.95), Inches(5.45))
        card.fill.solid()
        card.fill.fore_color.rgb = WHITE
        card.line.color.rgb = BORDER
        head = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(x + 0.18), Inches(1.45), Inches(5.5), Inches(0.35))
        p = head.text_frame.paragraphs[0]
        p.text = sec_title
        p.font.size = Pt(20)
        p.font.bold = True
        p.font.color.rgb = BLUE
        add_bullets(slide, sec_title, bullets, x=x + 0.18, y=1.85, w=5.45, h=4.6, font_size=17)
    add_footer(slide, slide_no)


def add_separator(paper_title, authors, year, slide_no):
    slide = prs.slides.add_slide(prs.slide_layouts[6])
    add_bg(slide, NAVY)
    rect = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.RECTANGLE, Inches(0.8), Inches(1.35), Inches(11.8), Inches(4.6))
    rect.fill.solid()
    rect.fill.fore_color.rgb = BLUE
    rect.line.color.rgb = ACCENT

    tb = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(1.2), Inches(1.8), Inches(10.9), Inches(1.6))
    p = tb.text_frame.paragraphs[0]
    p.text = paper_title
    p.font.size = Pt(30)
    p.font.bold = True
    p.font.color.rgb = WHITE

    sub = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(1.2), Inches(3.7), Inches(10.5), Inches(1.2))
    tf = sub.text_frame
    for i, line in enumerate([authors, f"Year: {year}"]):
        p = tf.paragraphs[0] if i == 0 else tf.add_paragraph()
        p.text = line
        p.font.size = Pt(20 if i == 0 else 18)
        p.font.color.rgb = WHITE
        p.space_after = Pt(8)
    add_footer(slide, slide_no)


def add_content_slide(title, bullets, slide_no, subtitle=None):
    slide = prs.slides.add_slide(prs.slide_layouts[6])
    add_bg(slide)
    add_header_bar(slide, title, subtitle)
    card = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.ROUNDED_RECTANGLE, Inches(0.45), Inches(1.2), Inches(12.35), Inches(5.8))
    card.fill.solid()
    card.fill.fore_color.rgb = WHITE
    card.line.color.rgb = BORDER
    add_bullets(slide, title, bullets, x=0.7, y=1.5, w=11.8, h=5.2, font_size=18)
    add_footer(slide, slide_no)


def add_timeline_overview(slide_no):
    slide = prs.slides.add_slide(prs.slide_layouts[6])
    add_bg(slide)
    add_header_bar(slide, "Overview: Technical Evolution Across Papers", "Cross-paper relationship")

    years = [
        ("2018", "GPT", "Pretrain + fine-tune", GOOD),
        ("2019", "GPT-2", "Zero-shot multitask transfer", ACCENT),
        ("2020", "GPT-3", "Few-shot / in-context learning", WARN),
        ("2024", "GPT-4", "Multimodal + alignment + predictable scaling", BLUE),
    ]
    line = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.RECTANGLE, Inches(1.2), Inches(3.55), Inches(10.8), Inches(0.08))
    line.fill.solid()
    line.fill.fore_color.rgb = NAVY
    line.line.color.rgb = NAVY
    xs = [1.5, 4.0, 6.5, 9.1]
    for (year, label, desc, color), x in zip(years, xs):
        circ = slide.shapes.add_shape(MSO_AUTO_SHAPE_TYPE.OVAL, Inches(x), Inches(3.15), Inches(0.55), Inches(0.55))
        circ.fill.solid()
        circ.fill.fore_color.rgb = color
        circ.line.color.rgb = WHITE
        tb = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(x - 0.2), Inches(2.05), Inches(1.1), Inches(0.35))
        p = tb.text_frame.paragraphs[0]
        p.text = year
        p.font.size = Pt(18)
        p.font.bold = True
        p.font.color.rgb = NAVY
        p.alignment = PP_ALIGN.CENTER
        lb = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(x - 0.55), Inches(3.95), Inches(1.7), Inches(1.3))
        tf = lb.text_frame
        for i, t in enumerate([label, desc]):
            p = tf.paragraphs[0] if i == 0 else tf.add_paragraph()
            p.text = t
            p.font.size = Pt(18 if i == 0 else 14)
            p.font.bold = i == 0
            p.font.color.rgb = TEXT
            p.alignment = PP_ALIGN.CENTER
    note = slide.shapes.add_textbox(Inches(0.8), Inches(5.95), Inches(11.9), Inches(0.65))
    p = note.text_frame.paragraphs[0]
    p.text = "Trend: less task-specific supervision, more scale, broader capability, and increasing emphasis on reliability and safety."
    p.font.size = Pt(18)
    p.font.color.rgb = TEXT
    p.alignment = PP_ALIGN.CENTER
    add_footer(slide, slide_no)


def build_deck():
    with open(STRUCTURE, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        structure = json.load(f)

    tz = timezone(timedelta(hours=8))
    gen_time = datetime.now(tz).strftime("%Y-%m-%d")

    # 1 cover
    add_title_only_slide(
        "Multi-Paper Analysis",
        [
            "Papers: GPT (2018) · GPT-2 (2019) · GPT-3 (2020) · GPT-4 Technical Report",
            f"Generation Date: {gen_time}",
            "Theme: Evolution of generative pretraining into multimodal aligned foundation models",
        ],
        1,
    )

    # 2 toc
    toc_rows = [
        ["1", "Overview, table of contents, and cross-paper comparison", "1-4"],
        ["2", "Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training", "5-14"],
        ["3", "Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners", "15-24"],
        ["4", "Language Models are Few-Shot Learners", "25-34"],
        ["5", "GPT-4 Technical Report", "35-44"],
        ["6", "Q&A", "45"],
    ]
    add_table_slide("Table of Contents", ["Part", "Content", "Slide Range"], toc_rows, 2, col_widths=[0.9, 9.5, 1.9])

    # 3 overview
    add_timeline_overview(3)

    # 4 comparison
    comp_rows = [
        ["GPT (2018)", "Pretrain + fine-tune", "Text", "BooksCorpus", "Transfer learning"],
        ["GPT-2 (2019)", "Zero-shot prompting", "Text", "WebText", "Emergent multitask behavior"],
        ["GPT-3 (2020)", "Zero/one/few-shot prompting", "Text", "CC + WebText2 + Books + Wiki", "In-context learning"],
        ["GPT-4 (2024)", "Aligned assistant-style prompting", "Text + Image", "Public + licensed data", "Predictable scaling + safety"],
    ]
    add_table_slide(
        "Cross-Paper Method Comparison",
        ["Paper", "Task Interface", "Modalities", "Training Data", "Key Theme"],
        comp_rows,
        4,
        col_widths=[2.2, 3.0, 1.5, 3.3, 2.2],
    )

    slides = []

    # Paper 1
    add_separator(
        "Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training",
        "Alec Radford · Karthik Narasimhan · Tim Salimans · Ilya Sutskever",
        "2018",
        5,
    )
    add_content_slide("Paper 1 · Basic Information", [
        "First GPT paper establishing generative pretraining as a practical transfer-learning method for NLP.",
        "Authors: Alec Radford, Karthik Narasimhan, Tim Salimans, Ilya Sutskever.",
        "Venue: OpenAI preprint (2018).",
        "Research position: bridge from task-specific NLP systems to reusable language-model backbones."
    ], 6, "GPT (2018)")
    add_content_slide("Paper 1 · Research Background and Motivation", [
        "NLP systems depended heavily on labeled data and hand-crafted architectures for each task.",
        "Word embeddings helped, but higher-level transfer remained inconsistent across benchmarks.",
        "Goal: learn universal linguistic features from unlabeled text, then adapt efficiently with minimal task-specific changes.",
        "Key question: can generative language modeling produce representations useful for many downstream tasks?"
    ], 7, "GPT (2018)")
    add_content_slide("Paper 1 · Core Contributions", [
        "Introduced the pretrain-then-fine-tune recipe for NLP using a decoder-only Transformer.",
        "Showed a single pretrained backbone can outperform specialized architectures on many tasks.",
        "Used task-aware input transformations to express NLI, QA, similarity, and classification in one token sequence framework.",
        "Reported state-of-the-art gains on 9 of 12 tasks.",
        "Demonstrated that adding auxiliary LM loss during fine-tuning improves adaptation stability."
    ], 8, "GPT (2018)")
    add_two_column_slide(
        "Paper 1 · Methods / Model Architecture",
        "Model",
        [
            "12-layer decoder-only Transformer",
            "768 hidden size, 12 attention heads",
            "3072-d feed-forward inner layer",
            "Autoregressive next-token pretraining objective",
        ],
        "Training / Transfer",
        [
            "Pretrained on BooksCorpus (7,000+ books)",
            "Long contiguous text helps learn long-range dependencies",
            "Fine-tuning adds lightweight output layer",
            "Auxiliary LM objective retained during supervised adaptation",
        ],
        9,
        "GPT (2018)"
    )
    add_table_slide(
        "Paper 1 · Experimental Setup",
        ["Task Group", "Datasets", "Metric / Notes"],
        [
            ["NLI", "SNLI, MultiNLI, QNLI, RTE, SciTail", "Mostly accuracy"],
            ["QA / Commonsense", "RACE, Story Cloze", "Accuracy"],
            ["Similarity", "MSRP, QQP, STS-B", "Correlation / accuracy"],
            ["Classification", "SST-2, CoLA", "Accuracy / Matthews"],
        ],
        10,
        col_widths=[2.4, 6.5, 3.0],
        subtitle="GPT (2018)"
    )
    add_table_slide(
        "Paper 1 · Experimental Results",
        ["Highlight", "Reported Outcome", "Interpretation"],
        [
            ["Story Cloze", "+8.9% absolute improvement", "Strong commonsense transfer"],
            ["RACE", "+5.7% absolute improvement", "LM pretraining helps QA reasoning"],
